questions.q: The Indian National Congress was a dominant party from 1947 to 1980. What was the main reason for its dominance?
questions.answer: The main reason for the Congress Party's dominance was its ability to adapt to the changing needs of the people and its strong leadership.

questions.q: Who is known as the 'architect of the one-party system' in India?
The one-party system in India was characterized by a dominant party and a weak opposition. What were the consequences of this situation?
questions.answer: The consequences of the one-party system in India were a lack of competition, a lack of accountability, and a decline in democratic values.

questions.q: What was the role of the Congress Party in the establishment of the one-party system in India?
questions.answer: The Congress Party played a crucial role in the establishment of the one-party system in India by winning a large majority of seats in the first general elections and then consolidating its power through various means.

questions.q: Who were the main critics of the one-party system in India?
questions.answer: Jayaprakash Narayan and Ram Manohar Lohia

questions.q: What was the impact of the one-party system on Indian democracy?
questions.answer: The one-party system had a negative impact on Indian democracy as it led to a lack of competition, a lack of accountability, and a decline in democratic values.

questions.q: How did the Congress Party maintain its dominance in the one-party system?
questions.answer: The Congress Party maintained its dominance by winning elections, consolidating its power, and suppressing opposition.

questions.q: Who is known as the 'father of the one-party system' in India?
What were the main features of the one-party system in India?
questions.answer: The main features of the one-party system in India were a dominant party, a weak opposition, and a lack of competition.

questions.q: How did the one-party system affect the opposition parties in India?
questions.answer: The one-party system had a negative impact on opposition parties in India as they were weak, fragmented, and lacked resources.
